text: The surgeon carefully identifies the duodenum, which is visually attached to the posterior aspect of the inflammatory tumor in patients with fistulizing Crohn's disease.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the inflammatory tumor by opening and dissecting the mesentery, liberating the terminal ileum and cecum, and fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon performs end-block exteriorization of the specimen through a midline incision, revealing the excised tissue.
The video shows the placement of ostomies through the midline abdominal wall, highlighting the surgical approach for ostomy creation.
Following the completion of the dissection and mini laparotomy, the surgeon prepares to perform the NM block exteriorization of the specimen through the midline incision.
The surgeon lengthens the diameter of the midline incision to accommodate the large inflammatory tumor, which involves multiple bowel segments.
The surgeon performs ligation of the thickened intestinal mesentery, which is visibly affected by the disease, highlighting the increased risk of postoperative bleeding at this level.
The surgeon prepares to perform a primary anastomosis in lateral fashion using a surgical stapler, creating a wide mouth to potentially reduce the risk of postoperative stenosis.

The surgeon addresses a short stenosis in the proximal small intestine by making a longitudinal incision through all bowel layers at the stricture site. The incision is then closed transversely with separate reabsorbable stitches, potentially performing multiple strictureplasties simultaneously.

The surgeon performs Finney's technique for treating longer strictures, creating an enterotomy and opening a healthy bowel loop adjacent to the stenosed segment. A mechanical suture is applied, mimicking a lateral-lateral anastomosis.
